title: Compose State Mastery
description: All state patterns: @Stable, @Immutable, SnapshotStateList, state hoisting, and when to use what

references.title: Android Developers — State and Jetpack Compose

lesson:
The Complete Guide to Compose State

State management in Compose has many tools. Choosing the right one depends on where the state lives, who needs it, and how it changes.

How remember { mutableStateOf() } Actually Works

There are two separate mechanisms working together:

Step 1: remember — Survival

Compose builds a slot table — a positional data structure indexed by call position. remember stores a value in a slot:

// Simplified internal pseudo-code of remember: @Composable fun <T> remember(calculation: () -> T): T { val position = currentComposer.currentPosition if (slotTable.hasValueAt(position)) { return slotTable.getValueAt(position) // recomposition: return stored } else { val value = calculation() // first composition: compute slotTable.setValueAt(position, value) return value } }

Step 2: mutableStateOf — Observation + Trigger

remember alone has no change detection. mutableStateOf() creates a MutableState that plugs into Compose's Snapshot system:

// Simplified internal pseudo-code: class SnapshotMutableState<T>(initial: T) { private var _value = initial var value: T get() { // RECORD: "this composable READS this state" Snapshot.current.recordRead(this) return _value } set(new) { if (new != _value) { // ← THE DIFF (structural equality) _value = new // NOTIFY: "all readers need to recompose!" Snapshot.current.notifyWrite(this) } } }

Why You Need BOTH

What You WriteSurvives Recomposition?Triggers Recomposition?
var x = 0NoNo
val x = remember { 0 }YesNo
val x = mutableStateOf(0)No (recreated each time!)Yes (but lost)
var x by remember { mutableStateOf(0) }YesYes
Two levels of diffing: (1) mutableStateOf diffs the value (old != new → schedule recomposition). (2) After recomposition, Compose diffs the UI tree output (old nodes vs new nodes → only patches changes).

State Hoisting vs Local State

The decision tree:

  • Local state (remember) — UI-only state no one else needs (expanded/collapsed, animation progress)
  • Hoisted state (passed as params) — state the parent needs to know about (selected item, text input)
  • ViewModel state (StateFlow) — business logic state that survives rotation (user data, API results)

How Compose Decides to Skip Recomposition

Compose skips recomposing a function when ALL its parameters are stable AND unchanged. A type is stable when:

  • It's a primitive (Int, String, Boolean, Float, etc.)
  • It's a data class where all properties are stable types
  • It's annotated with @Stable or @Immutable
  • It's a function type (lambdas) — but captured mutable state makes them unstable!
// STABLE — Compose can skip recomposition data class User(val name: String, val age: Int) // UNSTABLE — List is an interface, Compose can't guarantee immutability data class UserList(val users: List<User>) // FIX 1: Annotate with @Immutable (you promise it won't change) @Immutable data class UserList(val users: List<User>) // FIX 2: Use kotlinx.collections.immutable data class UserList(val users: ImmutableList<User>)

All Three Patterns: Click a Card to Select It

The same UI, three different architectures. Each trades simplicity for performance:

Pattern 1: State Hoisting + @Immutable Data (Standard / ViewModel)

Parent owns all state. Children are stateless — they receive data down and send events up.

// The data is @Immutable — never changes after creation @Immutable data class Card(val id: String, val title: String, val isSelected: Boolean = false) // ViewModel owns the list class CardViewModel : ViewModel() { private val _cards = MutableStateFlow(listOf( Card("1", "Kotlin"), Card("2", "Compose"), Card("3", "Room") )) val cards = _cards.asStateFlow() fun toggleCard(id: String) { _cards.update { list -> list.map { if (it.id == id) it.copy(isSelected = !it.isSelected) else it } } // Creates a NEW list with a NEW Card object for the clicked item } } @Composable fun CardList(viewModel: CardViewModel) { val cards by viewModel.cards.collectAsStateWithLifecycle() LazyColumn { items(cards, key = { it.id }) { card -> CardItem( title = card.title, isSelected = card.isSelected, onClick = { viewModel.toggleCard(card.id) } // event goes UP ) } } }
Recomposition flow: toggleCard() → new list via .copy() → StateFlow emits → CardList recomposes (reads StateFlow) → LazyColumn diffs items by key → Only changed Card reference → that CardItem recomposes → Unchanged Cards: SKIPPED (@Immutable + same reference)

Pattern 2: SnapshotStateList + @Immutable Items (Local / No ViewModel)

The list itself is Compose-observable. Replace items by index — Compose detects per-index changes.

@Immutable data class Card(val id: String, val title: String, val isSelected: Boolean = false) @Composable fun CardList() { val cards = remember { mutableStateListOf( Card("1", "Kotlin"), Card("2", "Compose"), Card("3", "Room") )} // mutableStateListOf = SnapshotStateList (Compose-observable) LazyColumn { items(cards.size, key = { cards[it].id }) { index -> val card = cards[index] CardItem( title = card.title, isSelected = card.isSelected, onClick = { cards[index] = card.copy(isSelected = !card.isSelected) // Replaces ONE item. SnapshotStateList detects index-level change } ) } } }
Recomposition flow: cards[0] = card.copy(...) → SnapshotStateList records write at index 0 → Only composables that READ index 0 recompose → That one CardItem recomposes → Other indices: UNTOUCHED

Pattern 3: @Stable Wrapper with mutableStateOf (Self-Mutating Items)

Each item manages its own observable state. No callbacks, no new objects, no parent involvement.

@Stable class CardState( val id: String, val title: String, isSelected: Boolean = false ) { var isSelected by mutableStateOf(isSelected) // observable! private set fun toggleSelected() { isSelected = !isSelected } } @Composable fun CardList() { val cards = remember { listOf( CardState("1", "Kotlin"), CardState("2", "Compose"), CardState("3", "Room") )} // Just a regular List — the ITEMS are observable, not the list LazyColumn { items(cards, key = { it.id }) { card -> CardItem( title = card.title, isSelected = card.isSelected, // reads mutableStateOf onClick = { card.toggleSelected() } // mutates in place! ) } } }
Recomposition flow: card.toggleSelected() → isSelected.value changes → Snapshot system checks: who READ this State object? → Only CardItem that called card.isSelected → recomposes → List: unchanged. Other cards: UNTOUCHED. → No new objects created. No parent involved. This is the NARROWEST possible recomposition scope.

Pattern Comparison

PatternState Lives InChange MechanismRecompositionBest For
1. State Hoisting
@Immutable + ViewModel		ViewModel StateFlow.copy() → new listParent + changed itemStandard MVVM, API data
2. SnapshotStateList
@Immutable items		Composable (remember)list[i] = .copy()Changed index onlyLocal lists, no ViewModel
3. @Stable Wrapper
mutableStateOf fields		Inside each itemProperty mutation in placeChanged property reader onlyComplex per-item state
When to use which:
• Pattern 1 when state is tied to business logic (API responses, database, ViewModel) — most common in production apps
• Pattern 2 when state is local UI-only and you don't need a ViewModel — quick prototypes, local editable lists
• Pattern 3 when items have complex independent state (selected + expanded + editing + animation) — most efficient for per-item reactivity

questions.title: Why List<T> is Unstable
questions.description: Why does Compose treat List<User> as unstable, even when you pass the same list?
options: Lists are always slow in Compose
List<T> is an interface — Compose cannot guarantee the implementation is immutable. Someone could pass a MutableList cast as List, and it could change between recompositions
Compose only supports arrays, not lists
Lists are stable but only in debug builds
questions.explanation: List<T> is a Kotlin interface. The actual object could be an ArrayList (mutable) passed as a List reference. Compose can't guarantee it won't be mutated externally between recompositions, so it marks it unstable and always recomposes. Fixes: (1) @Immutable annotation (your promise), (2) ImmutableList from kotlinx-collections-immutable (compiler-verified), (3) toList() to create a snapshot.
hints: Think about Kotlin interfaces vs implementations — List could be backed by MutableList
Compose needs guarantees about immutability to skip safely

questions.title: Clickable Cards — State Hoisting
questions.description: This card list doesn't update when cards are clicked. The click should toggle a "selected" visual state. Fix it using state hoisting.

checks.message: Use a Compose-observable collection (mutableStateSetOf or mutableStateOf(setOf()))

questions.explanation: A plain mutableSetOf() is NOT observable by Compose — mutating it does not trigger recomposition. Fix: use mutableStateOf(setOf()) and create a new set on each update (using + and - operators). Alternatively, use mutableStateSetOf() which is a Compose-observable set. The key insight: Compose only recomposes when it can detect the change.
hints: Regular Kotlin collections are invisible to Compose recomposition
You need either a State wrapper or a Compose snapshot collection

questions.title: SnapshotStateList
questions.description: What happens when cards[0] = cards[0].copy(isClicked = true) is called?
options: Nothing — SnapshotStateList doesn't trigger recomposition
Only the clicked card recomposes (because of keys + SnapshotStateList detects index-level changes)
The entire LazyColumn recomposes
The app crashes — you can't modify a list during composition
questions.explanation: mutableStateListOf() creates a SnapshotStateList — a list that Compose observes at the element level. When you replace an item at index 0, only composables reading that index recompose. Combined with key = { cards[it].id }, LazyColumn knows exactly which item changed and only recomposes that one CardItem. This is the most efficient pattern for local mutable lists.
hints: SnapshotStateList tracks reads and writes at the index level
Keys tell LazyColumn which items are which

questions.title: @Stable Class with Internal State
questions.description: Create a @Stable CardState class where each card manages its own observable selected/expanded state. The composable should NOT need a callback — the card updates itself.
questions.explanation: The @Stable annotation tells Compose this class is safe to skip during recomposition IF the reference hasn't changed. Inside, mutableStateOf properties are individually observable — when isSelected changes, only composables reading that specific property recompose. This is the "per-item state" pattern: each card manages its own state without needing callbacks to a parent. The parent just holds a list of CardState objects.
hints: @Stable tells Compose: trust me, this object is well-behaved
Each mutableStateOf property is independently observable

questions.title: @Immutable vs @Stable
questions.description: What is the difference between @Immutable and @Stable?
options: They are the same annotation
@Immutable = object never changes after creation (all properties are val). @Stable = object may change, but Compose will be notified of changes (via mutableStateOf). Both enable skipping
@Immutable is for classes, @Stable is for functions
@Stable is deprecated in favor of @Immutable
questions.explanation: @Immutable is a stronger guarantee: the object's publicly visible properties will NEVER change. All properties should be val with immutable types. @Stable is weaker: properties CAN change, but changes are always observable (via mutableStateOf). Both tell Compose it's safe to skip recomposition based on reference equality. Use @Immutable for pure data, @Stable for stateful objects.
hints: Think about whether the object can change after creation
Both enable smart recomposition, but make different promises

questions.title: Lambda Causing Unnecessary Recomposition
questions.description: Every time the parent recomposes, ALL items in this LazyColumn recompose — even unchanged ones. The lambda is the culprit. Fix it.

checks.message: Stabilize the lambda with remember or extract the handler

questions.explanation: The lambda { viewModel.toggleTask(task.id) } captures viewModel (unstable) — Compose treats it as a new lambda on every recomposition. Wrapping the handler in remember creates a stable reference. Since viewModel never changes identity (ViewModel survives recomposition), it's safe to remember. This prevents all TaskItems from recomposing when count changes.
hints: A lambda that captures unstable objects is itself unstable
If the captured reference never changes, you can remember the lambda

questions.title: Choosing the Right State Pattern
questions.description: You have a list of cards rendered in a LazyColumn. Clicking a card should toggle its "selected" visual. Describe at least three different patterns to implement this and when to use each.
questions.answer: Pattern 1: State Hoisting — Parent holds selectedIds as mutableStateOf(setOf()). Pass isSelected boolean and onClick callback down. Best for: simple lists where parent needs to know selections (e.g., bulk delete). Pattern 2: SnapshotStateList — Use mutableStateListOf and replace items with .copy(isSelected = !isSelected). Changes at index level trigger per-item recomposition. Best for: local UI lists without a ViewModel. Pattern 3: @Stable wrapper — Create a @Stable class with mutableStateOf properties and toggle methods. Each item manages its own state, no callbacks needed. Best for: complex per-item state (selected + expanded + editing) where many independent properties change.
questions.explanation: All three patterns work, but optimize differently. State hoisting follows the standard Compose pattern (state down, events up) and works naturally with ViewModels. SnapshotStateList gives granular reactivity for local lists. @Stable wrappers are most efficient for complex per-item state because only the changed property triggers recomposition. Choose based on: Who needs the state? How complex is per-item state? Is there a ViewModel?
hints: Think about where state lives: parent, list, or individual items
Each pattern trades simplicity for performance differently

questions.title: derivedStateOf Misuse
questions.description: This code uses derivedStateOf incorrectly. The filtered list never updates. Fix it.

checks.message: derivedStateOf must be able to read the current state values, not captured initial values

questions.explanation: Actually, this code DOES work correctly! derivedStateOf reads items and filterText as State objects inside the lambda — Compose tracks these reads and re-runs the derivation when they change. The by delegation is the main fix — using .value inside the LazyColumn items call also works. The key insight: derivedStateOf tracks State reads, not variable captures. As long as items and filterText are backed by State (they are — collectAsStateWithLifecycle and mutableStateOf), the derivation updates correctly. Common misunderstanding: people think remember "freezes" the values, but derivedStateOf reads State objects reactively.
hints: derivedStateOf tracks reads of State objects — it's reactive, not a one-time capture
Make sure you're reading .value (State) not a captured snapshot

questions.title: remember(key) vs remember
questions.description: What does providing a key to remember(key) do?
options: The key makes it thread-safe
remember(key) recreates the value when the key changes. remember (no key) keeps the initial value forever
The key is used for debugging only
remember(key) is deprecated
questions.explanation: remember (no key) creates the value once and keeps it for the lifetime of the composable in the composition. remember(key) invalidates and recreates the value when the key changes. If userId changes, Option B creates a new DateFormatter with the new user. Option A would keep using the old one. Use keys when the remembered value depends on a parameter.
hints: What if the user navigates to a different profile? Should the formatter stay the same?

questions.title: @Stable Self-Mutating Card
questions.description: What happens when the user clicks the first card? Trace the recomposition path.
options: All three CardItems recompose because the list changed
Only CardItem for "Kotlin" recomposes — its mutableStateOf(isSelected) changed, and Compose only tracks readers of that specific State object. The list itself is unchanged.
Nothing recomposes because the list reference did not change
The app crashes because you cannot mutate @Stable objects
questions.explanation: @Stable tells Compose: "trust this object's snapshot-observable properties." When card.toggleSelected() flips isSelected, the mutableStateOf snapshot system notifies only the composable that read that property — the CardItem at position 0. The list reference is unchanged, the other CardState objects are untouched, so cards 2 and 3 are completely skipped. This is the most granular recomposition possible.
hints: mutableStateOf tracks individual readers — who called .get() on this State?
The list itself never changes — no new list, no .copy()

questions.title: @Stable Todo Item with Multiple States
questions.description: Create a @Stable TodoItemState class with: title (fixed), isCompleted (toggleable), isExpanded (toggleable), and note (editable). Then write a composable that uses it — no callbacks to parent needed.

checks.message: Use by delegation for clean property access

questions.explanation: Each mutableStateOf property is independently observable. Checking the checkbox only recomposes composables reading isCompleted. Expanding only recomposes composables reading isExpanded. Typing in the note only recomposes the TextField area. No callbacks to parent, no new list, no .copy(). The TodoItem composable is fully self-contained. Use private set on properties that should only be changed through methods (encapsulation).
hints: Each mutableStateOf property triggers recomposition independently
private set means external code must use the toggle methods, not set directly

questions.title: @Stable with ViewModel Integration
questions.description: This code tries to use @Stable states with a ViewModel but the states are lost on configuration change (rotation). Fix it.

checks.message: Keep the state in the ViewModel where it survives rotation

questions.explanation: remember does NOT survive configuration changes — only rememberSaveable and ViewModel state do. Move the CardState list into the ViewModel, which survives rotation. The @Stable objects inside still work — mutableStateOf properties are still independently observable. The ViewModel holds the list reference (StateFlow), and each item manages its own UI state internally. Best of both worlds: ViewModel lifecycle + per-item granular reactivity.
hints: remember is lost on rotation — what survives configuration changes?
The @Stable pattern still works even when the objects live in a ViewModel

questions.title: Three Approaches Compared
questions.description: You have a list of 100 cards in a LazyColumn. The user clicks one card to select it. Which approach causes the least recomposition?
options: State hoisting: parent holds selectedId StateFlow, passes isSelected boolean to each CardItem. On click, update StateFlow with new ID → entire list recomposes to re-evaluate isSelected for each item.
SnapshotStateList with @Immutable items: cards[i] = cards[i].copy(isSelected = true). Only the changed index recomposes (with keys).
@Stable wrapper: card.toggleSelected() mutates mutableStateOf internally. Only the clicked card's composable recomposes — Compose tracks the exact State reader.
All three cause the same recomposition
questions.explanation: @Stable wrapper wins for minimal recomposition. State hoisting recomposes the parent (which re-evaluates all items). SnapshotStateList recomposes only the changed index but still creates a new item object. @Stable with mutableStateOf has the narrowest scope: only the composable that reads isSelected on that specific object recomposes. No parent involvement, no new objects, no list changes. For 100 items, this means 1 recomposition vs potentially 100.
hints: Think about what triggers each recomposition and how many composables are affected
Who "reads" the changed state in each approach?

questions.title: @Immutable vs @Stable Contract
questions.description: What is the contract difference between these two approaches, and what could go wrong with each?
options: They are identical — @Immutable and @Stable mean the same thing

@Immutable promises NOTHING changes after creation — if you violate this (e.g., var inside), Compose may show stale UI. @Stable promises changes are OBSERVABLE via snapshot system — safe to mutate, but you MUST use mutableStateOf for mutable fields.
@Immutable is faster than @Stable
@Stable is deprecated
questions.explanation:
@Immutable = "I will NEVER change. All properties are val with immutable types." Compose can skip recomposition purely on reference equality. If you lie (put a var or mutable collection inside), Compose won't detect changes → stale UI, subtle bugs.

@Stable = "I might change, but ALL changes go through mutableStateOf, so Compose will be notified." Safer for mutable objects. If you put a plain var (without mutableStateOf) inside a @Stable class, Compose won't see those changes either.

Rule: Every mutable property in a @Stable class MUST be by mutableStateOf().
hints: What happens if you put a plain var inside an @Immutable class?
Both annotations are promises to the Compose compiler — what are you promising?
